food_name: Char Siu Bao — Baked (Glazed Pastry BBQ Pork Bun)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for baked char siu bao. Closest parent would be a filled baked bun or pastry category. No exact URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Filled buns and rolls — baked; closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for an enriched baked filled bun. No exact code exists for char siu bao specifically.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for baked char siu bao. A039S (filled buns and rolls) is the closest defensible parent category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard baked char siu bao weighs approximately 75g and provides 270 kcal, 12g fat, and 335mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: Char Siu Bao (baked) — Health Promotion Board Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for baked char siu bao. Values represent per 100g edible portion.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of enriched wheat-flour baked buns — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for enriched bread and filled pastry products
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for baked char siu bao in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of approximately 65 (medium) is estimated by analogy with enriched wheat-flour baked buns and filled pastry products in the database. The fat matrix from lard/butter and eggs moderates the glycemic response relative to plain white bread (GI ~70). GL per 75g serving calculated as: (65 × 30.8g available carbohydrate per 75g) / 100 = approximately 20. Confidence 0.9.

hypertension.guidance: Each baked char siu bao contributes approximately 335mg sodium (14% of the 2300mg daily limit); patients with hypertension should limit to one bun and avoid pairing with other high-sodium dim sum items such as siu mai or har gow in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI approximately 65 (medium), GL approximately 20 per 75g bun; 270 kcal per bun with 12g fat
type2_diabetes.guidance: The baked version has a marginally lower GI (~65) than the steamed version (~70) due to the fat matrix, but delivers 270 kcal per bun — significantly more than the steamed version (185 kcal); patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to one bun, account for the GL of 20 in their carbohydrate budget, and avoid treating it as a light snack, as two buns equal 540 kcal and a GL of 40.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6g per 100g (4.5g per bun); cholesterol 55mg per 100g; trans fat 0.1g per 100g from lard or shortening
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The enriched pastry dough containing lard or butter contributes 4.5g saturated fat and trace trans fat per bun; patients with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to one bun occasionally and prefer the steamed version (2.3g saturated fat per 100g) as a lower-saturated-fat alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Baked char siu bao (焗叉燒包) is the golden, glossy-topped Hong Kong bakery version of the classic Cantonese BBQ pork bun. Unlike the steamed version, which has a soft white dough exterior, the baked version features an enriched pastry shell made with wheat flour, lard or butter, eggs, and sugar, producing a slightly flaky, crumbly texture similar to a soft pastry. The exterior is coated with an egg wash glaze before baking, which turns a deep amber-gold in the oven, creating the characteristic shiny appearance. The filling is identical to the steamed version — diced or minced char siu (BBQ pork) seasoned with soy sauce, oyster sauce, hoisin sauce, sesame oil, and sugar. One standard bun weighs approximately 75g and provides 270 kcal, 12g fat (4.5g saturated), 30.8g carbohydrate, 8.3g protein, and 335mg sodium. The baked version is significantly more calorie-dense than the steamed version (185 kcal per bun) due to the fat-enriched dough. GI is approximately 65 (medium), with a glycemic load of approximately 20 per bun. Key clinical message: many patients prefer the baked version for its palatability and appearance — the counselling priority is limiting to one bun and not treating it as a light snack. Two baked buns equal 540 kcal and 670mg sodium, equivalent to a full meal. Widely sold at Singapore Chinese bakeries, hotel yum cha, and dedicated dim sum restaurants.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (foodo.sg), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The food is an exact match in the HPB database. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database with confidence 0.9, which is the highest available for this food type.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for baked char siu bao is not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source. trans_fat_g is 0.1g per 100g as reported by HPB, reflecting trace trans fat from lard or shortening used in the pastry dough. Two portions are listed in the input (75g and 85g); the 75g portion is used as the default serving size per the input record designation. The 85g variant reflects larger commercial bakery portions. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct measurement for baked char siu bao exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 75g serving calculated as (65 × 30.825) / 100 = 20.0 (rounded). FoodEx2 code A039S assigned as closest defensible parent; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for baked char siu bao. Halal status is not confirmed as the filling contains pork.
